source: Predictability Limits in Session-based Next Item Recommendation.pdf

concepts.finding:
We study seven benchmarks for session-based recommendation, 
covering retail, music and news domains to investigate how accu­
rately user behavior can be predicted from the session histories.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
The problem of making recom­
mendations tailored to user sessions, rather than established users, 
is called session-based recommendation.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
While the quality in recommendations goes beyond 
just being able to fnd the most likely item, this is still an important 
indicator in determining whether the model has captured user 
interests accurately.
concepts.implication: Can inform intent modeling for recommendation systems

concepts.finding:
Diferences in methodology, baselines and evaluation datasets 
in session-based recommendation research have made compari­
son and analysis of diferent algorithmic approaches difcult.
concepts.implication: Inform search ranking and retrieval optimization

concepts.finding:
Purely session-based recommendation algorithms should not be 
expected to improve above these values.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
On the other hand, where the exact prediction 
or the top position in a ranked list of recommendations is important, 
the performance will be directly bound by these limits.
concepts.implication: General ML methodology applicable to content recommendation

concepts.finding:
The estimates of predictabil­
ity for other metrics that consider multiple recommendations and 
ranking positions, like the mean reciprocal rank (MRR) would be 
of practical value.
concepts.implication: Inform search ranking and retrieval optimization

concepts.finding:
The algorithm-specifc analysis should be ex­
tended to important sequential recommendation methods – matrix 
factorization and neural networks.
